body_txt: A fictional actor in the universe of the work is mentioned as having played a role in a Real Life work (usually film or television, but occasionally theatre). This has its uses: it can be an easy shorthand to establish the Famed in Story level of a character or a fandom that may have built up around them in the fictional universe if they're working from a known template. It's also commonly used to invoke History with Celebrity if the cast apparently stayed the same except for one or two changes for fictional purposes. Writing Around Trademarks examples or expies usually don't count. This is when it's explicitly mentioned to be the same work (though, as noted above, the fidelity to the real-world cast list may vary greatly depending on what's needed for the work). This might be one way of avoiding a Celebrity Paradox . Cases where the actor appears as themselves don't count, no matter how different or exaggerated they may be from their real self. Be careful with Small Reference Pools . References to, for instance, Shakespeare plays or roles are common both because of Small Reference Pools and because a lot of Shakespeare is put on worldwide. A Shakespearean example would need to very clearly reference a known production. Compare Fictional Fan, Real Celebrity and Almost Famous Name for other times when "celebrity" reality and fiction brush up against each other. In visual works, it will commonly include Starring a Star as a Star , and may also include an Actor Allusion (or several) and/or a Casting Gag .
